select_when: The same facts are being interpreted through different human worlds shaped by norms, status, pressure, or local context.
Adoption matters as much as analysis, and a recommendation will fail unless it is translated into the language and motivations of the people who must act.
Leaders need to separate stereotype from situated truth and understand whether behavior is driven by role, culture, pressure, or history.
A diverse group or cross-context team needs better perspective taking before it can coordinate effectively.
avoid_when: Cultural understanding is being used as a polite label for untested assumptions about motives, resistance, or alignment.
The problem is being reduced to culture talk when the real issue is a concrete incentive, role, or process failure that should be named directly.

curation_notes.summary: Kept the model on context-specific interpretation, stereotype avoidance, and adoption-relevant translation rather than broad diversity rhetoric.
donor_drops: Did not widen into generic inclusion language because the raw source is strongest on perspective taking, situated truth, and anti-stereotype discipline.
open_questions: The output label compresses organizational, cultural, and interpersonal surfaces into one coordination-focused artifact.

select_when.note: The strengths section explicitly supports different human worlds, adoption-sensitive translation, stereotype avoidance, and diverse-team coordination.

avoid_when.note: Both avoid conditions come directly from the raw warning about cultural understanding becoming an untested assumption label.

input_type.note: The source activates around cross-context interpretation and coordination, while the compact type label is reviewed normalization.

output_type.note: The playbook yields a context-aware read of how coordination and adoption will work; the output label compresses that surface.

reasoning_types.note: The model diagnoses situated human differences and reasons systemically about how those differences affect organizational outcomes.
